title: Latent tire-state features from a state-space filter with pit resets
summary: A 2026 F1 tire-degradation paper models degradation as a latent stochastic state with explicit pit-stop resets and shows rolling-origin robustness across race sessions.
rationale: A 2026 F1 tire-degradation paper models degradation as a latent stochastic state with explicit pit-stop resets and shows rolling-origin robustness across race sessions. That is a better inductive bias than static ratios like `DegPerLap` and `TyreLifeToRaceLeft`, especially when degradation accelerates or becomes noisy.
implementation_hint: Fit a lightweight per-stint filter/state-space approximation using observed lap time, lap delta, compound, and pit resets; export posterior latent pace, latent degradation rate, change-in-rate, reset gap, and posterior uncertainty. Use these as features in the existing XGB/Cat models rather than replacing the tabular stack.
expected_effect: Adds interpretable hidden-state features that can separate true tire fade from transient noise, weather, or traffic artifacts.
risk: A full Bayesian fit may be too slow; use a simplified Kalman/state update or offline prefit parameters first. Poorly estimated latent states can underperform simpler handcrafted features.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
